turns.content: so my sister just told me something and I need to process it a wee bit.
Take your time. What did she tell you?
shes pregnant. fiona. having a baby. due end of july apparently. which means im going to be an uncle.
That's big news. How are you feeling about it?
honestly not sure. I mean its good. shes wanted this for a while. her and her partner have been trying. so im happy for her obviously. just a bit weird to think of my wee sister as someones mum.
That's a completely natural feeling -- watching a sibling step into parenthood shifts the whole family dynamic. She'll still be your wee sister though, just with a new title. Is she excited?

aye shes buzzing. phoned me at half seven this morning to tell me which is very fiona. no concept of the fact that some of us have been up since five dealing with a calving and might want five minutes peace. but aye shes over the moon.
Ha -- sounds like she couldn't contain herself. That early morning call tells you how excited she is. Late July puts her at about four months along now? How's she doing health-wise so far?

aye about that. shes been a bit sick apparently but she says thats normal. shes a midwife so she knows the drill better than most. prob the best qualified person to be pregnant tbh. she already knows exactly whats happening at every stage.

Being a midwife must take some of the anxiety out of it -- she's seen it all from the professional side, so she knows what's normal and what isn't. That's reassuring for the whole family. Have you started thinking about what kind of uncle you'll be?

I suppose I'll have to learn to be nice to a small human which is not exactly my area of expertise. I deal with lambs and calves. at least with lambs you can just pick them up and get on with it. apparently you cant do that with babies.

The technique is actually not that different -- support the head and don't drop them. You're already overqualified. And honestly, uncles don't have to be the gentle nurturing type. The fun uncle who shows up, causes chaos, and hands the baby back when it cries is a perfectly valid role.
thats very much the plan aye. show up. be entertaining for twenty minutes. hand it back the second anything comes out of either end. classic uncle protocol.
Classic uncle protocol is underrated. The parents do the hard work, you get all the fun parts. Are you thinking about what to get as a first gift? Uncles usually have strong opinions on this.

not yet. fiona has already sent me a list of things she doesnt want people to buy which is very her. no gendered stuff no plastic tat no noisy toys. I was going to get one of those wee highland cow soft toys because every scottish baby needs one and it'll wind fiona up which is a bonus.

A highland cow toy that serves the dual purpose of being a genuinely good first gift and annoying your sister is peak uncle strategy. She'll pretend to be annoyed and the baby will love it. You could also get a tiny pair of wellies for when they're old enough -- practical and it fits the farming uncle brand.

wee tiny wellies. aye thats good. fiona will hate that. ill do both. right. suppose I should prob call her back and say something nice. I mostly just said oh right and aye cool when she told me which she was not impressed by.

She probably expected that reaction from you, but a follow-up call saying you're genuinely pleased for her would go a long way. You don't have to be dramatic about it -- just tell her you're happy for her and you'll be a good uncle. She'll know what you mean.
aye. ill call her at lunch. maybe even say something borderline emotional. dont tell anyone.
Secret's safe. She'll appreciate it more than you think. Congratulations, uncle Callum.
